evals.prompt: Create a skill that runs our smoke tests after a deploy and summarizes failures. It's an action, ops tier. Name it whatever's compliant.
evals.expected_output: Happy path. The skill confirms the brief, proposes a compliant verb-style name (e.g. `smoke-test` or `check-deploy`) plus an alternative, and scaffolds the skill folder: a SKILL.md whose frontmatter name equals the folder name, a what+when description, and a metadata.skillroy block (phase, tier: ops, version), plus references/ and an evals/ stub. It does not invent a canonical token; if a domain token is needed it asks. It hands off with the phase and what would take it to publish.
expectations: A skill folder is scaffolded with a SKILL.md, a references/ directory, and an evals/ stub.
The SKILL.md frontmatter `name` exactly equals the new folder name and is kebab-case.
The frontmatter contains a metadata.skillroy block with phase, tier (ops), and version.
The proposed name is a verb/verb-phrase (action skill) and does not repeat the word 'skill' or the repo/domain.
The description is written as what-it-does + when-to-use-it (trigger phrases), not just a label.
No canonical/domain token is invented; if one is needed the skill asks rather than guessing.

evals.expected_output: Under-specified invocation, tests elicitation. Because nothing was specified, the skill does NOT scaffold anything yet. It asks for the brief: what the skill does and when it should trigger, whether it's an action or knowledge skill, the tier, the domain/canonical token (if any), and the phase. Then it waits for the user.
expectations: No skill folder or files are created during this turn.
The skill asks what the new skill should do and when it should trigger (the what + when).
The skill asks whether it is an action (verb) or knowledge (noun) skill, and for the tier.
The skill asks for the phase (brainstorming / adhoc / publish).
The skill establishes where the skill will live (which collection, or the parent location for a new one) rather than silently choosing a destination.
The skill does not fabricate a name, tier, or canonical token on the user's behalf.

evals.expected_output: Non-compliant name, tests naming enforcement. The skill flags that the proposed name violates conventions (uppercase, underscores, a version baked into the name) and proposes a compliant kebab-case alternative (e.g. `deploy-helper`), explaining that names are API contracts and versions belong in metadata.skillroy.version, not the name. It only scaffolds once a compliant name is agreed.
expectations: The skill explicitly identifies that 'MyDeploy_Helper_v2' is non-compliant (uppercase and/or underscores and/or a version in the name).
The skill proposes a compliant kebab-case [a-z0-9-] alternative name.
The skill notes that the version belongs in metadata.skillroy.version (or a tag), not in the name.
The skill does not create a folder literally named 'MyDeploy_Helper_v2'.

evals.prompt: Create an ops skill for our frobnicator service. Use the domain token `frobnicator`.
evals.expected_output: Canonical-token discipline. `frobnicator` is not in the collection's token catalog. The skill does NOT silently accept or invent it: it surfaces that the token isn't in the catalog and asks how to proceed (add it to the catalog first, choose an existing token, or confirm it's intentional), rather than scaffolding with a fabricated token.
expectations: The skill checks the supplied domain token against the collection's catalog rather than accepting it blindly.
The skill reports that `frobnicator` is not a known canonical token.
The skill asks how to proceed (e.g. register the token in the catalog, or pick an existing one) instead of inventing/guessing.
The skill does not finalize a scaffold that bakes in an unvetted canonical token without flagging it.

evals.prompt: I'm starting a brand-new project for our widget data utilities -- there's no repo yet. Create it with a first skill that reshapes widget exports. Tier dx, domain 'widgets'... call the project whatever's compliant.
evals.expected_output: New collection + first skill in one flow. The skill settles a compliant <tier>-<domain> collection name (e.g. dx-widgets), scaffolds the collection shell (README with the §10 metadata block at status: experimental, .claude/skills/ home, the safe .gitignore, .gitattributes, .agents/skills symlink) via new-collection.py --with-skill, with the skill inheriting tier dx. It offers git init as a follow-up (the script is git-free) and checks 'widgets' against the token catalog if one is supplied rather than asserting it.
expectations: A compliant <tier>-<domain> collection name is proposed (not the user's words verbatim if non-compliant).

The parent location for the new collection is decided by the user in the question itself (proposed with rationale, not stated in a preamble and acted on); any write outside the invoking repo gets explicit sign-off first.

The collection shell is scaffolded: README containing a collection metadata block with status: experimental, a .claude/skills/ home, a .gitignore that does not swallow the skills home, and the .agents/skills symlink.
The first skill is scaffolded inside the new collection's home and inherits the collection's tier.
git init is offered as a follow-up step, not run by the scaffolder.
The domain token is validated against a catalog when available, or surfaced as needing one -- not silently asserted.

evals.prompt: Set up a new skills project called Widget_Utils_Tools for the team.
evals.expected_output: Non-compliant collection name. The skill flags Widget_Utils_Tools (uppercase, underscores, redundant -Tools suffix, no tier) and proposes a compliant <tier>-<domain> alternative (asking for the tier if unclear). It scaffolds only once a compliant name (or an explicit brand-name exception) is agreed; new-collection.py itself also rejects the non-compliant form.
expectations: The skill identifies the name as non-compliant: case, underscores, and the redundant -Tools suffix are called out, and the missing tier is raised.
A compliant <tier>-<domain> alternative is proposed, with the tier elicited if not stated.
No folder named Widget_Utils_Tools is created.
The brand-name escape (--brand) is mentioned only as an explicit, deliberate exception, not applied silently.
